title: Hash Embeddings for Efficient Word Representations
authors: Dan Svenstrup (DTU), Jonas Meinertz Hansen (FindZebra), Ole Winther (DTU)
venue: NeurIPS 2017 (31st Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems); arXiv:1709.03933
tokenization_method: Hash embedding: each token w is represented by k (default k=2) component vectors selected from a SHARED pool of B trainable vectors via k independent uniform hash functions H_1..H_k, plus a token-specific k-dimensional learnable 'importance parameter' vector p_w. The final d-dim token vector is the weighted sum e_hat_w = p_w^T c_w where c_w = (H_1(w),...,H_k(w)). Two layers of hashing: D1: token->id in {1..K} (a dictionary OR a hash fn), then D2: id->bucket in {1..B} (the actual collision-inducing hash). The embedding matrix E is B x d (shared codebook); the importance matrix P is K x k. Optionally p_w is concatenated to e_hat_w. It is explicitly an interpolation between a standard embedding (B=|T|, identity hash) and the pure hashing trick (k=1, p=1).
feature_types_handled: categorical / discrete tokens only (words and n-grams). No numerical, temporal, or missing-value mechanism — it is an NLP word-representation method. Maps onto tabular ONLY via the high-cardinality categorical leg (treating each category value as a 'token').
token_granularity: per-value (per distinct word / n-gram). NOT per-field. One token = one vocabulary item; the representation is a learned weighted combination of k shared codebook entries plus a per-token importance vector.
numerical_strategy: none (out of scope for the paper; continuous features not addressed).
categorical_strategy: hashing trick with learnable collision repair: instead of one embedding per category, share a pool of B vectors across all categories via k hashes, and learn k per-category importance weights that combine the hashed vectors. Decouples parameter count from vocabulary/cardinality K.
high_cardinality_handling: THIS IS THE CORE CONTRIBUTION. Handles vocabularies of millions-to-100M tokens with B<<|T|. Parameters = B*d + K*k vs standard K*d, so adding one new category costs only k (=2) params instead of d (=10..300). The k-hash scheme makes the effective bucket space B^k, cutting total-collision probability dramatically (worked example: |T|=100M, B=1M, k=2 drops per-token collision prob from ~1.0 to ~1e-4). Importance parameters perform IMPLICIT vocabulary pruning by driving unimportant tokens' weights toward 0, and let two colliding-but-both-important tokens still be separated in the k-dim subspace spanned by their distinct hash picks.
scales_to_many_features: Scales in CARDINALITY (vocabulary size), not in number of features. The whole motivation is a single huge token space; parameter count grows as B*d + K*k, essentially independent of K's growth (only +k per new id). It does NOT model many distinct heterogeneous fields or feature interaction — the document embedding is just a sum of token embeddings fed to a shallow/deep softmax classifier. No attention, no per-field structure.
downstream_model: Bag-of-n-grams: token embeddings summed into a document vector, then either (5.3) a single softmax layer, or (5.4) 3 dense layers (1000 ReLU units) + batchnorm + softmax. Also a 10-model soft-voting ensemble (5.5). Keras + TensorFlow.
pretraining_objective: none / supervised end-to-end. Embeddings and importance parameters trained jointly by cross-entropy on the classification target (Adam, lr 1e-3, early stopping). Future-work floats pre-training distributable 'lean hash vectors' as a word2vec alternative, but not done.
key_results: 7 Zhang-et-al text-classification datasets (AG, DBPedia, Yelp P/F, Yahoo, Amazon F/P). Without dictionary (shallow net): hash emb. matches or beats standard hashing-trick embeddings on all 7 except DBPedia (98.5 vs 98.6) while using 40M vs 200M params (5x fewer). With dictionary (deep net): hash emb. uses ~3x fewer params on average and beats standard embeddings on most datasets, dramatically so on Yahoo (71.3 vs 65.8) and Yelp full (62.6 vs 61.4) — interpreted as a regularizing effect from the smaller bucket count. Ensemble (~150M params, vs 200M standard) reaches top-3 of SOTA on 5 of 7 datasets. Marginally FASTER to train than standard embeddings at large vocab.
baselines_compared: Standard hashing-trick embedding (fastText/Joulin-2016b style, 10M buckets, 200M params); standard full-vocabulary learned embedding; and the SOTA table of char-CNN, char-CRNN, VDCNN, wordCNN, Discriminative LSTM, virtual-adversarial net, fastText, BoW, n-grams, n-grams-TFIDF.
transferable_idea: Replace per-category learned embedding tables (entity embeddings) with a SHARED component-vector pool selected by k hashes plus per-category learnable importance weights — giving collision-tolerant, dictionary-free embeddings for arbitrarily high-cardinality categorical fields at a parameter cost that is near-independent of cardinality (+k params per new value vs +d). The importance-weight trick is the reusable novelty: it repairs the rare-but-important-value collisions that make the naive single-hash trick lossy.
limitations: (1) Only ~1-2 percentage-point gains over the plain hashing trick on most datasets; the headline 'better than standard embeddings' rests partly on a regularization effect that a smaller standard vocab could also buy. (2) k and the index function D-hat fixed to D1 in all experiments; the (claimed-better) different-hash-for-importance and concatenation aggregations are left to future work. (3) Importance params P are still K x k = one row per id, so if D1 is itself a hash with collisions, two ids sharing a first-layer bucket get IDENTICAL component vectors AND importance weights — i.e. truly indistinguishable (authors flag this). (4) Bucket count B is the regularization knob but is chosen by cross-validation, not principled. (5) Evaluated only on balanced BOW text classification.
pitfalls: (a) The 'orders-of-magnitude parameter reduction' assumes a genuinely huge single vocabulary; for a tabular feature with cardinality < ~10*B, hashing buys nothing over a plain entity-embedding table and just adds collision risk. (b) Most of the gain reported is over the HASHING TRICK, not over a well-sized standard embedding — on the without-dict comparison hash emb. and standard hashing are near-ties on most datasets; do not over-read the win. (c) Collisions are reduced, not eliminated; a rare-but-critical category (e.g. a fraud-signal merchant ID) can still share both vectors and importance with a common one when first-layer D1 collides. (d) The method has NO numerical / temporal / missing handling and no feature-interaction modeling — it is one leg of a tokenizer, not a tokenizer for 70+ mixed features.
relevance_to_topic: The canonical mechanism for the high-cardinality categorical leg of a 70+-mixed-feature tokenizer: it lets million-cardinality ID fields (user/item/SKU/merchant) be embedded with a bounded parameter budget and no pre-built dictionary, with learnable importance weights repairing collisions — directly transferable as a drop-in replacement for entity-embedding tables on the few genuinely high-cardinality columns, while the numerical/temporal legs need other methods.
code_available: no official repo from the authors found; the algorithm is simple (B x d pool + K x k weights + k hashes) and has community reimplementations (e.g. in PyTorch and in the dl-with-keras book repos). Verify before relying.
